infobox.value: * At-Large: Noel DiBona * At-Large: Anne Mahoney (president) * At-Large: Ziqiang Yuan * Ward 1: David Jacobs * Ward 2: Richard Ash * Ward 3: Walter Hubley * Ward 4: Virginia Ryan * Ward 5: Maggie McKee * Ward 6: Deborah Riley

(English pronunciation: [/ˈkwɪnzi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Noaius_Paticus-Quincy,_Massachusetts.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Noaius Paticus-Quincy, Massachusetts.wav")) is a [city](/wiki/City "City") in [Norfolk County](/wiki/Norfolk_County,_Massachusetts "Norfolk County, Massachusetts"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), United States. It is the largest city in the county. Quincy is part of the [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") area as one of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")'s immediate southern [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"). Its population in 2020 was 101,636, making it the [seventh-largest city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Massachusetts#Largest_cities "List of municipalities in Massachusetts") in the state.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Known as the "City of Presidents",[[3]](#cite_note-3) Quincy is the birthplace of two [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States")—[John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") and his son [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams")—as well as [John Hancock](/wiki/John_Hancock "John Hancock"), the first signer of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and the first and third governor of Massachusetts.

**Quincy** (English pronunciation: [/ˈkwɪnzi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Noaius_Paticus-Quincy,_Massachusetts.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Noaius Paticus-Quincy, Massachusetts.wav")) is a [city](/wiki/City "City") in [Norfolk County](/wiki/Norfolk_County,_Massachusetts "Norfolk County, Massachusetts"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), United States. It is the largest city in the county. Quincy is part of the [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") area as one of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")'s immediate southern [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"). Its population in 2020 was 101,636, making it the [seventh-largest city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Massachusetts#Largest_cities "List of municipalities in Massachusetts") in the state.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Known as the "City of Presidents",[[3]](#cite_note-3) Quincy is the birthplace of two [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States")—[John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") and his son [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams")—as well as [John Hancock](/wiki/John_Hancock "John Hancock"), the first signer of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and the first and third governor of Massachusetts.

First settled in 1625, Quincy was briefly part of [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Boston "Dorchester, Boston") before becoming the North Precinct of [Braintree](/wiki/Braintree,_Massachusetts "Braintree, Massachusetts") in 1640. In 1792, Quincy was split off from [the Town of Braintree](/wiki/Town_of_Braintree "Town of Braintree") and was [incorporated](/wiki/Incorporated_community#English-speaking "Incorporated community") separately as the Town of Quincy; the new town was named after Colonel [John Quincy](/wiki/John_Quincy "John Quincy"), maternal grandfather of [Abigail Adams](/wiki/Abigail_Adams "Abigail Adams") and after whom John Quincy Adams was also named.[[4]](#cite_note-herring-4) Quincy became a city in 1888.

For over a century, Quincy was home to a thriving [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [quarrying](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry") industry; the city was also the site of the [Granite Railway](/wiki/Granite_Railway "Granite Railway"), the United States' first commercial railroad. Shipbuilding at the [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard") was another key contributor to the city's economy. In the 20th century, both [Howard Johnson's](/wiki/Howard_Johnson%27s "Howard Johnson's") and [Dunkin' Donuts](/wiki/Dunkin%27_Donuts "Dunkin' Donuts") were founded in the city. Today, Quincy has developed into both a vibrant immigrant destination and a suburban business hub, and is considered attractive due to its safety, relatively large housing stock, and extensive connections to Boston.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-Colonial Period to the Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-Colonial Period to the Revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Mount_Wollaston_sketch.jpg/250px-Mount_Wollaston_sketch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Wollaston_sketch.jpg)

View of Mount Wollaston as it appeared in 1840, virtually unchanged from the time of initial English settlement in 1625. The central part of this sketch was adopted as the seal of Quincy.

The road that eventually became the Old Coast Road from Boston to [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"), going through Quincy and Braintree, started out as a Native American trail.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Massachusett](/wiki/Massachusett "Massachusett") [Sachem](/wiki/Sachem "Sachem") [Chickatawbut](/wiki/Chickatawbut "Chickatawbut") had his seat on a hill called [Moswetuset Hummock](/wiki/Moswetuset_Hummock "Moswetuset Hummock") prior to the settlement of the area by English colonists, situated east of the mouth of the [Neponset River](/wiki/Neponset_River "Neponset River") near what is now called [Squantum](/wiki/Squantum "Squantum").[[6]](#cite_note-tcpl-hummock-6) It was visited in 1621 by [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") commander [Myles Standish](/wiki/Myles_Standish "Myles Standish") and [Squanto](/wiki/Squanto "Squanto"), a native guide.[[7]](#cite_note-neal-history-of-ne-7)

Four years later, a party led by Captain [Richard Wollaston](/wiki/Richard_Wollaston "Richard Wollaston") established [a post on a low hill](/wiki/Merrymount_Colony "Merrymount Colony") near the south shore of [Quincy Bay](/wiki/Quincy_Bay "Quincy Bay") east of present-day Black's Creek. The settlers found the area suitable for farming, as Chickatawbut and his group had cleared much of the land of trees. (The Indians used the name Passonagessit ("Little Neck of Land") for the area.[[8]](#cite_note-enotes-8)) This settlement was named Mount Wollaston in honor of the leader, who left the area soon after 1625, bound for [Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia").[[9]](#cite_note-lodge-wollaston-9)

The [Wollaston](/wiki/Wollaston_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Wollaston (Quincy, Massachusetts)") neighborhood in Quincy still retains Captain Wollaston's name. Upon the departure of Wollaston, [Thomas Morton](/wiki/Thomas_Morton_(colonist) "Thomas Morton (colonist)") took over leadership of the post. Morton's history of conflict with the Plymouth settlement and his free-thinking ideals antagonized the Plymouth settlement, who maligned the colony and accused it of [debauchery](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/debauchery "wikt:debauchery") with Indian women and [drunkenness](/wiki/Alcohol_intoxication "Alcohol intoxication").[[9]](#cite_note-lodge-wollaston-9) Morton renamed the settlement Ma-re-Mount ("Hill by the Sea") and later wrote that the conservative [separatists](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)") of [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") to the south were "threatening to make it a woefull mount and not a merry mount", in reference to the fact that they disapproved of his [libertine](/wiki/Libertine "Libertine") practices.[[10]](#cite_note-morton-10) In 1627, Morton was arrested by Standish for violating the code of conduct in a way harmful to the colony. He was sent back to England, only to return and be arrested by [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") the next year.[[9]](#cite_note-lodge-wollaston-9) The area of Quincy now called [Merrymount](/wiki/Merrymount_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Merrymount (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is located on the site of the original English settlement of 1625 and takes its name from the punning name given by Morton.[[11]](#cite_note-merrymount_assoc-11)

The area was first incorporated as part of [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Boston "Dorchester, Boston") in 1630; it was briefly annexed by Boston in 1634.[[12]](#cite_note-cfa3-5-12) The area became [Braintree](/wiki/Braintree,_Massachusetts "Braintree, Massachusetts") in 1640,[[13]](#cite_note-pattee-13) bordered along the coast of Massachusetts Bay by Dorchester[[14]](#cite_note-dot-atheneum-14) to the north and [Weymouth](/wiki/Weymouth,_Massachusetts "Weymouth, Massachusetts")[[15]](#cite_note-weymouth-hist-15) to the east. Beginning in 1708, the modern border of Quincy first took shape as the North Precinct of Braintree.[[13]](#cite_note-pattee-13)

### Post-Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-Revolution")]

Following the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), in 1792, Quincy was officially incorporated as a separate town from Braintree, named for Col. [John Quincy](/wiki/John_Quincy "John Quincy"), the grandfather of Abigail Adams.[[16]](#cite_note-pattee-inc-16) It was made a city in 1888.[[17]](#cite_note-quincy-booklet-17) Quincy, Massachusetts, is the only one of the 17 U.S. cities named "[Quincy](/wiki/Quincy_(disambiguation)#United_States "Quincy (disambiguation)")" whose residents pronounce the name as "KWIN-zee" rather than "KWIN-see". In 1845, the [Old Colony Railroad](/wiki/Old_Colony_Railroad "Old Colony Railroad") opened; the [Massachusetts Historical Commission](/wiki/Massachusetts_Historical_Commission "Massachusetts Historical Commission") stated that the railroad was "the beginning of a trend toward [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization")." Quincy became as accessible to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") as was [Charlestown](/wiki/Charlestown,_Boston "Charlestown, Boston"). The first suburban land company, Bellevue Land Co., had been organized in northern Quincy in 1870.[[18]](#cite_note-mhc-quincy-18) Quincy's population grew by over 50 percent during the 1920s.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Among the city's several firsts was the [Granite Railway](/wiki/Granite_Railway "Granite Railway"), the first commercial railroad in the United States. It was constructed in 1826 to carry [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") from a [Quincy quarry](/wiki/Quincy_Quarries_Reservation "Quincy Quarries Reservation") to the [Neponset River](/wiki/Neponset_River "Neponset River") in [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Massachusetts "Milton, Massachusetts") so that the stone could then be taken by boat to erect the [Bunker Hill Monument](/wiki/Bunker_Hill_Monument "Bunker Hill Monument") in Charlestown. Quincy granite became famous throughout the nation, and [stonecutting](/wiki/Stonemasonry "Stonemasonry") became the city's principal economic activity. Quincy was also home to the first [iron furnace](/wiki/Iron_furnace "Iron furnace") in the United States, the [John Winthrop Jr. Iron Furnace Site](/wiki/John_Winthrop_Jr._Iron_Furnace_Site "John Winthrop Jr. Iron Furnace Site") (also known as Braintree Furnace), from 1644 to 1653.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Quincy_Masschusetts_Frederick_Childe_Hassam_1892.jpeg/250px-Quincy_Masschusetts_Frederick_Childe_Hassam_1892.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Quincy_Masschusetts_Frederick_Childe_Hassam_1892.jpeg)

*Quincy, Massachusetts*, oil on canvas, [Childe Hassam](/wiki/Childe_Hassam "Childe Hassam"), 1892

In the 1870s, the city gave its name to the [Quincy Method](/wiki/Quincy_Method "Quincy Method"), an influential approach to education developed by [Francis W. Parker](/wiki/Francis_Wayland_Parker "Francis Wayland Parker") while he served as Quincy's [superintendent of schools](/wiki/Superintendent_(education) "Superintendent (education)"). Parker, an early proponent of [progressive education](/wiki/Progressive_education "Progressive education"), put his ideas into practice in the city's underperforming schools; four years later, a state survey found that Quincy's students were excelling.[[20]](#cite_note-216.239.59.132-20) Many of Quincy's teachers were recruited by districts in other states, spreading the Quincy method beyond Massachusetts to New Jersey, New York, Connecticut, Vermont, Florida, Minnesota, and other places.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Quincy was additionally important as a [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") center. [Sailing ships](/wiki/Sailing_ship "Sailing ship") were built in Quincy for many years, including the only seven-masted [schooner](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner") ever built, [*Thomas W. Lawson*](/wiki/Thomas_W._Lawson_(ship) "Thomas W. Lawson (ship)"). The [Fore River](/wiki/Fore_River_(Massachusetts) "Fore River (Massachusetts)") area became a shipbuilding center in the 1880s. Founded by [Thomas A. Watson](/wiki/Thomas_A._Watson "Thomas A. Watson"), who became wealthy as assistant to [Alexander Graham Bell](/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell "Alexander Graham Bell") in developing the telephone, many famous [warships](/wiki/Warship "Warship") were built at the [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard"). Amongst these were the [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") [USS *Lexington* (CV-2)](/wiki/USS_Lexington_(CV-2) "USS Lexington (CV-2)"); the [battleships](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") [USS *Massachusetts* (BB-59)](/wiki/USS_Massachusetts_(BB-59) "USS Massachusetts (BB-59)"), now preserved as a [museum ship](/wiki/Museum_ship "Museum ship") at [Battleship Cove](/wiki/Battleship_Cove "Battleship Cove") in Massachusetts, and [USS *Nevada* (BB-36)](/wiki/USS_Nevada_(BB-36) "USS Nevada (BB-36)"); and [USS *Salem* (CA-139)](/wiki/USS_Salem_(CA-139) "USS Salem (CA-139)"), the world's last all-gun heavy warship, which is still preserved at Fore River as the main exhibit of the [United States Naval Shipbuilding Museum](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Shipbuilding_Museum "United States Naval Shipbuilding Museum"). [James J. Kilroy](/wiki/James_J._Kilroy "James J. Kilroy"), reputed originator of the famous [Kilroy was here](/wiki/Kilroy_was_here "Kilroy was here") graffiti, was a rivet inspector at the Fore River Shipyard.[[22]](#cite_note-osgood-kilroy-22)

Quincy was also an [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation") pioneer thanks to Dennison Field. Located in [Squantum](/wiki/Squantum "Squantum"), it was one of the world's first airports and was partially developed by [Amelia Earhart](/wiki/Amelia_Earhart "Amelia Earhart"). In 1910, it was the site of the [Harvard Aero Meet](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Aero_Meet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Harvard Aero Meet (page does not exist)"), the second air show in America. It was later leased to the [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") for an airfield, and served as a reserve [Squantum Naval Air Station](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Squantum "Naval Air Station Squantum") into the 1950s. The [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") has also long maintained a presence in the city, with the [Massachusetts Army National Guard](/wiki/Massachusetts_National_Guard "Massachusetts National Guard") occupying the Kelley Armory in Wollaston; from 1971 to 1976 it served as headquarters for the [187th Infantry Brigade](/wiki/187th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_States) "187th Infantry Brigade (United States)").

The [Howard Johnson's](/wiki/Howard_Johnson%27s "Howard Johnson's") and [Dunkin' Donuts](/wiki/Dunkin%27_Donuts "Dunkin' Donuts") restaurant chains were both founded in Quincy. [Celtic punk](/wiki/Celtic_punk "Celtic punk") band [Dropkick Murphys](/wiki/Dropkick_Murphys "Dropkick Murphys") got its start in the city's [Wollaston](/wiki/Wollaston_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Wollaston (Quincy, Massachusetts)") neighborhood in 1996. Quincy is also home to the United States' longest-running [Flag Day](/wiki/Flag_Day_(United_States) "Flag Day (United States)") parade, a tradition that began in 1952 under Richard Koch, a former director of City Parks and Recreation, who started the "Koch Club" sports organization for kids and held an annual parade with flags.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Quincy_MA_geography.png/250px-Quincy_MA_geography.png)](/wiki/File:Quincy_MA_geography.png)

Quincy and surrounding area showing elevations and features

Quincy shares borders with [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") to the north (separated by the [Neponset River](/wiki/Neponset_River "Neponset River")), [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Massachusetts "Milton, Massachusetts") to the west, [Randolph](/wiki/Randolph,_Massachusetts "Randolph, Massachusetts") and [Braintree](/wiki/Braintree,_Massachusetts "Braintree, Massachusetts") to the south, and [Weymouth](/wiki/Weymouth,_Massachusetts "Weymouth, Massachusetts") (separated by the [Fore River](/wiki/Weymouth_Fore_River "Weymouth Fore River")) and [Hull](/wiki/Hull,_Massachusetts "Hull, Massachusetts") (maritime border between [Quincy Bay](/wiki/Quincy_Bay "Quincy Bay") and [Hingham Bay](/wiki/Hingham_Bay "Hingham Bay")) to the east. Historically, before incorporation when it was called "Mount Wollaston" and later as the "North Precinct" of Braintree, Quincy roughly began at the Neponset River in the north and ended at the Fore River in the south.

Quincy Bay, within city limits to the northeast, is part of [Boston Harbor](/wiki/Boston_Harbor "Boston Harbor") and [Massachusetts Bay](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay "Massachusetts Bay"). There are several beaches in Quincy,[[24]](#cite_note-24) including [Wollaston Beach](/wiki/Wollaston_Beach "Wollaston Beach") along [Quincy Shore Drive](/wiki/Quincy_Shore_Drive "Quincy Shore Drive"). Located on the western shore of Quincy Bay, Wollaston Beach is the largest Boston Harbor beach.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Quincy's territory includes [Hangman Island](/wiki/Hangman_Island "Hangman Island"), [Moon Island](/wiki/Moon_Island_(Massachusetts) "Moon Island (Massachusetts)") (restricted access, and all land is owned by the City of Boston), [Nut Island](/wiki/Nut_Island "Nut Island") (now a peninsula), and [Raccoon Island](/wiki/Raccoon_Island_(Massachusetts) "Raccoon Island (Massachusetts)") in the [Boston Harbor Islands National Recreation Area](/wiki/Boston_Harbor_Islands_National_Recreation_Area "Boston Harbor Islands National Recreation Area").

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 26.9 square miles (70 km2), of which 16.8 square miles (44 km2) is land and 10.1 square miles (26 km2) is water. The total area is 37.60% water.

Although Quincy is primarily urban, 2,485 acres (3.9 sq mi; 10.1 km2)[[26]](#cite_note-26) or fully 23 percent of its land area lies within the uninhabited [Blue Hills Reservation](/wiki/Blue_Hills_Reservation "Blue Hills Reservation"), a state park managed by the [Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation](/wiki/Department_of_Conservation_and_Recreation_(Massachusetts) "Department of Conservation and Recreation (Massachusetts)"). This undeveloped natural area encompasses the southwestern portion of Quincy and includes the city's highest point, 517-foot (158 m) Chickatawbut Hill. Other hills within Quincy include Forbes Hill in Wollaston, Presidents Hill in Quincy Center and Penns Hill in South Quincy.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[47]](#cite_note-:0-47) there were 101,636 people and 46,789 households, making it the eighth-largest city in the state. The population density was 6,137.6 inhabitants per square mile (2,369.7/km2). There were 51,156 housing units.

The [racial makeup](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_Census "Race and ethnicity in the United States Census") of the city was 56.2% [White](/wiki/White_people "White people") (non-Hispanic), 6.4% [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") alone, 0.1% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") alone, 28.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") alone (15.6% [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans"), 3.2% [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_Americans "Vietnamese Americans"), 2.6% [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans")), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") or [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander"), 0.85% from other races, and 5.3% from two or more races.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-47) [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race were 5.4% of the population. 33.5% were of Irish, 12.7% Italian and 5.0% English ancestry according to the [2000 Census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"). 58.1% spoke only English,[[48]](#cite_note-48) while 8.0% spoke [Chinese or Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), 2.6% [Cantonese](/wiki/Standard_Cantonese "Standard Cantonese"), 1.9% Spanish, 1.5% Vietnamese and 1.3% Italian in their homes.

Of the city's 46,789 households, approximately 56.6% were married couples living together, 12.5% had a female householder with no spouse present, 5.2% were male householders with no spouse present, and 25.8% were non-families. 50.1% of Quincy's population was male, and 49.9% female. The average household size was 2.2 people, and most people (84.3%) were in the same house a year ago.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-47)[[49]](#cite_note-:1-49)

In the city, the age distribution of the population shows 14% under the age of 18, 66.2% from 18 to 64, and 19.9% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 40.8 years.[[49]](#cite_note-:1-49)

The median income for a household in the city was $90,668. Males had a median income of 1.31 times greater than females ($97,905 compared to $74,737 for females). The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $53,082. ### Asian community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Asian community")]

See also: [Chinese Americans in Boston](/wiki/Chinese_Americans_in_Boston "Chinese Americans in Boston")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Kam_Man_Food.jpg/250px-Kam_Man_Food.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kam_Man_Food.jpg)

[Kam Man Food](/wiki/Kam_Man_Food "Kam Man Food") in Quincy

As of 2010, Quincy has the highest per capita concentration of persons of Asian origin in Massachusetts.[[50]](#cite_note-Encarnacaosurging-50) As of 2003 about 66% of the Asians in Quincy are [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans"),[[51]](#cite_note-EschbacherIndian-51) giving the city one of the largest Chinese populations in the state.[[52]](#cite_note-hsiaoWeekend-52) There is also a community of persons [of East Indian origins](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans"), with most of them working in [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") and other skilled professions.[[51]](#cite_note-EschbacherIndian-51) A growing number of people with [Vietnamese origins](/wiki/Vietnamese_Americans "Vietnamese Americans") live in the area as well and make up the second largest Asian American group in Quincy; it is estimated that nearly 4,000 Vietnamese people live in the city.

In 1980, there were 750 persons of Asian origin in Quincy. Most of the Asian immigrants coming in the 1980s originated from Hong Kong and Taiwan.[[53]](#cite_note-Eschbachergrowingchanging-53) In 1990, Quincy had 5,577 persons of Asian origin,[[50]](#cite_note-Encarnacaosurging-50) with 143 of them being of East Indian origin.[[51]](#cite_note-EschbacherIndian-51) The number of Asians increased to 13,546 in 2000,[[50]](#cite_note-Encarnacaosurging-50) with about 9,000 of them being ethnic Chinese,[[52]](#cite_note-hsiaoWeekend-52) and 1,127 of them being ethnic East Indian. The latter group grew by 688%, making it the fastest-growing Asian subgroup in Quincy.[[51]](#cite_note-EschbacherIndian-51) Around 2003, most Asian immigrants were coming from [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") instead of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[53]](#cite_note-Eschbachergrowingchanging-53) At that time, Quincy had a higher Asian population than the Boston Chinatown.[[54]](#cite_note-EschbacherNextChinatown-54) The overall Asian population increased by 64% in the following decade, to 22,174 in 2010.[[50]](#cite_note-Encarnacaosurging-50) Quincy's Chinese population increased by 60% during that time period.[[55]](#cite_note-FoxChinesesuburbs-55)

Historically, Quincy's Asian residents traveled to shops in [Chinatown, Boston](/wiki/Chinatown,_Boston "Chinatown, Boston").[[54]](#cite_note-EschbacherNextChinatown-54) In 2003, New York City-based [Kam Man Food](/wiki/Kam_Man_Food "Kam Man Food") opened one of its supermarkets in Quincy.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In February 2017, City Councilor Nina Liang presented a motion to designate Quincy as a "Sanctuary City".[[57]](#cite_note-57) This motion was voted down by the City Council. Quincy has an estimated 8,000 undocumented residents and has the 11th-highest concentration of immigrants in Massachusetts overall.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

As of 2000, about 50% of Asians in Quincy own their own houses; many who rent do so while saving money for down payments for their houses.[[53]](#cite_note-Eschbachergrowingchanging-53) Sixty-five percent of the Chinese were homeowners, while only 10% of the East Indians were homeowners.[[51]](#cite_note-EschbacherIndian-51) As of 2003, slightly more than 2,500 Asian Americans in Quincy were registered to vote, making up almost 25% of Asians in the city who were eligible to vote.[[59]](#cite_note-AsianFuture-59)

In the 1980s, the city experienced unrest due to racial tensions and violence directed toward Southeast Asian and Chinese residents.[[60]](#cite_note-TensionsAsians-60) At this time, the Quincy Police Department did not employ any [AAPI](/wiki/Asian_Pacific_Americans "Asian Pacific Americans") police officers, which led to a lack of trust within the [Asian-American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") community.[[61]](#cite_note-OfficersAsians-61) The City gradually increased its outreach to its Asian-American communities and developed multicultural programming showcasing immigrant cultures to help familiarize the larger community with its new neighbors and promote community integration. Racial tensions gradually diminished, and by 2003,[[60]](#cite_note-TensionsAsians-60) the Quincy Police Department had prioritized the diversification of their force, employing multiple Asian-American officers .[[61]](#cite_note-OfficersAsians-61)

In 2003, Quincy Asian Resources Inc. planned to establish a newsletter for Asian residents.[[59]](#cite_note-AsianFuture-59) In 2011, Boston Chinatown Neighborhood Center, Inc. (BCNC; 波士頓華埠社區中心) began offering services in Quincy.[[55]](#cite_note-FoxChinesesuburbs-55)

## Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Quincy_MA_Neighborhoods.png/250px-Quincy_MA_Neighborhoods.png)](/wiki/File:Quincy_MA_Neighborhoods.png)

Map of Quincy neighborhoods

Quincy is divided into numerous [neighborhoods](/wiki/Neighbourhood "Neighbourhood") with individual histories and characteristics.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

* [Adams Shore](/wiki/Adams_Shore "Adams Shore") was originally developed as a summer [resort](/wiki/Resort "Resort") location and is now a year-round [residential area](/wiki/Residential_area "Residential area").
* [Germantown](/wiki/Germantown_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Germantown (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is the site of a former planned manufacturing community begun in the 1750s to encourage [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration"), it is now a densely populated residential neighborhood featuring several public housing developments.
* [Houghs Neck](/wiki/Houghs_Neck "Houghs Neck") is a northeastern peninsular community named for Atherton Hough, who was granted the land in 1636 for use as a farm and [orchard](/wiki/Orchard "Orchard"). Hough's Neck has a substantial Irish-American population.
* [Marina Bay](/wiki/Marina_Bay_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Marina Bay (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is a residential-[commercial](/wiki/Commercial_district "Commercial district") area developed in the 1980s on the site of the closed [Naval Air Station Squantum](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Squantum "Naval Air Station Squantum") with [high-rise](/wiki/High-rise "High-rise") [condominiums](/wiki/Condominium_(living_space) "Condominium (living space)"), restaurants and a large [marina](/wiki/Marina "Marina").
* [Merrymount](/wiki/Merrymount_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Merrymount (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is a primarily residential neighborhood and the site of Quincy's initial settlement.
* [Montclair](/wiki/Montclair_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Montclair (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is the northwestern section of the city along West Squantum Street, bordering the town of [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Massachusetts "Milton, Massachusetts").
* [North Quincy](/wiki/North_Quincy_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "North Quincy (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is a residential and commercial neighborhood along Hancock Street and Quincy Shore Drive that is home to a substantial [Asian-American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") population, the community is regionally notable for its culturally diverse small business sector, and it is the location of the city's largest high school, [North Quincy High School](/wiki/North_Quincy_High_School "North Quincy High School").
* [Quincy Center](/wiki/Quincy_Center "Quincy Center") is a historic commercial and government center of the city where [City Hall](/wiki/Quincy_City_Hall "Quincy City Hall"), [Thomas Crane Public Library](/wiki/Thomas_Crane_Public_Library "Thomas Crane Public Library"), the [United First Parish Church](/wiki/United_First_Parish_Church "United First Parish Church") (Old Stone Church), [Quincy Masonic Building](/wiki/Masonic_Temple_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Masonic Temple (Quincy, Massachusetts)"), [The Adams Academy](/wiki/Adams_Academy "Adams Academy") building, [The Woodward School for Girls](/wiki/Woodward_School_for_Girls "Woodward School for Girls"), and numerous office buildings and residential streets can be found.
* [Quincy Point](/wiki/Quincy_Point "Quincy Point") is a densely populated residential area east of Quincy Center, with commercial areas along Quincy Avenue and [Southern Artery](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3A "Massachusetts Route 3A"), it is also the site of the [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard").
* [South Quincy](/wiki/South_Quincy "South Quincy") is a residential and commercial area bordering the town of [Braintree](/wiki/Braintree,_Massachusetts "Braintree, Massachusetts") that includes Crown Colony office park and Faxon Park, a wooded 66-acre (0.27 km2) protected space.
* [Squantum](/wiki/Squantum "Squantum") is a [peninsular](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") area and the northernmost region of Quincy, once-popular as a summer seaside resort destination, it is now a year-round residential area known for its tight-knit community and its recreational offerings, including several municipal beaches and [Squantum Point Park](/wiki/Squantum_Point_Park "Squantum Point Park"), which is on the site of the former [Naval Air Station Squantum](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Squantum "Naval Air Station Squantum"). Squantum has a significant Irish-American population.
* [West Quincy](/wiki/West_Quincy_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "West Quincy (Quincy, Massachusetts)") is a residential and commercial section with immediate access to [Interstate 93](/wiki/Interstate_93 "Interstate 93") and is the site of several former [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [quarries](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry"), now the [Quincy Quarries Reservation](/wiki/Quincy_Quarries_Reservation "Quincy Quarries Reservation"), as well as the [Granite Railway](/wiki/Granite_Railway "Granite Railway"), the first commercial railway in the United States.
* [Wollaston](/wiki/Wollaston_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Wollaston (Quincy, Massachusetts)"), named for Captain Richard Wollaston, the leader of Quincy's original settlers, was an early rail-accessed commuter home for Boston workers, it is now a densely populated residential and commercial area that includes the former [Eastern Nazarene College](/wiki/Eastern_Nazarene_College "Eastern Nazarene College") campus.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![A brown 10-story office building, headquarters building of Stop & Shop supermarket chain in Quincy Center](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Stop_%26_Shop_Headquarters.jpg/250px-Stop_%26_Shop_Headquarters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stop_%26_Shop_Headquarters.jpg)

Headquarters building of [Stop & Shop](/wiki/Stop_%26_Shop "Stop & Shop") supermarket chain in Quincy Center

During its history, Quincy has been known as a manufacturing and heavy industry center, with [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [quarrying](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry") dominating employment in the 19th century and [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") at the Fore River Shipyard and Squantum Victory Yard rising to prominence in the 20th century. The recent decades have seen a shift in focus to several large employers in the professional and service sector of the economy.[[63]](#cite_note-jabaily-frb-63) Quincy is the location of the corporate headquarters of several firms, including [Boston Financial Data Services](/w/index.php?title=Boston_Financial_Data_Services&action=edit&redlink=1 "Boston Financial Data Services (page does not exist)"),[[64]](#cite_note-64) the [Stop & Shop](/wiki/Stop_%26_Shop "Stop & Shop") [supermarket](/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket") chain,[[65]](#cite_note-65) [Arbella Insurance Group](/wiki/Arbella_Insurance_Group "Arbella Insurance Group")[[66]](#cite_note-MajorEmp-66) and *[The Patriot Ledger](/wiki/The_Patriot_Ledger "The Patriot Ledger")*, the publisher of the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Massachusetts) "South Shore (Massachusetts)")'s largest regional newspaper.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Other major employers with offices in Quincy are [State Street Corporation](/wiki/State_Street_Corporation "State Street Corporation"),[[68]](#cite_note-68) [Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts](/wiki/Blue_Cross_Blue_Shield_of_Massachusetts "Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts"),[[69]](#cite_note-69) [Harvard Pilgrim Health Care](/wiki/Harvard_Pilgrim_Health_Care "Harvard Pilgrim Health Care")[[66]](#cite_note-MajorEmp-66) and [Boston Scientific](/wiki/Boston_Scientific "Boston Scientific").[[66]](#cite_note-MajorEmp-66) [TACV](/wiki/Cabo_Verde_Airlines "Cabo Verde Airlines"), national [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") airline of [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), has its United States corporate office in Quincy.[[70]](#cite_note-70) [Icelandair](/wiki/Icelandair "Icelandair") has its North American headquarters in the city as well.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Quincy_City_Hall_2019.jpg/250px-Quincy_City_Hall_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quincy_City_Hall_2019.jpg)

Quincy City Hall in 2019

### Local

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Local")]

Quincy has a [strong mayor government](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government#Strong-mayor_form "Mayor–council government"). The incumbent mayor, [Thomas P. Koch](/wiki/Thomas_P._Koch "Thomas P. Koch"), has served since 2008; he is the [33rd mayor of the city](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Quincy,_Massachusetts "List of mayors of Quincy, Massachusetts"). Mayors in the city were elected to two-year terms. In 2013, the city's voters opted to extend the mayoral term to four years, beginning after the 2015 election.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

In addition to the mayor, the city has a nine-member city council, with Anne Mahoney serving as president as of 2026.[[76]](#cite_note-76) One councilor is elected to represent each of the city's six wards, and three are elected at large. Councilors serve two-year terms. The city also has a school committee with seven members—the mayor and six members elected to staggered four-year terms.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

#### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Public safety")]

The Quincy Police Department was formed in 1888, currently headquartered at the original [Quincy Police Station](/wiki/Quincy_Police_Station "Quincy Police Station") which was built in 1925 as the city's first purpose-built [police station](/wiki/Police_station "Police station") and added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1990. In 2010, the city of Quincy was the first in the US to have its police department carry the nasal spray [Narcan](/wiki/Narcan "Narcan") (Nalaxone) to combat the overdose outbreak associated with the [opioid epidemic in the US](/wiki/Opioid_epidemic_in_the_United_States "Opioid epidemic in the United States").[[78]](#cite_note-78) When the program first began, the city's officers were reviving an overdose victim every four to five days. By 2014, police officers had administered the opioid antagonist over 300 times. Other cities and police departments throughout the US developed their own Narcan-dispensing programs based on the model pioneered by the Quincy PD.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In 2017, overdose deaths in the city and the Commonwealth of Massachusetts had declined, it was thought, due to the use of naloxone by the police and others. The state legislature, in 2018, required all pharmacies to keep Narcan in stock and available to anyone, without a prescription.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Fire emergencies are handled by the Quincy Fire Department, which was founded in 1889 and includes the [Central Fire Station](/wiki/Central_Fire_Station_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Central Fire Station (Quincy, Massachusetts)"), which is on the National Register of Historic Places and is still active as of 2023.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The city's [Emergency Medical Services](/wiki/Emergency_Medical_Services "Emergency Medical Services") are privately contracted, with ambulance response being handled by Brewster Ambulance Service since 2015.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: State")]

Quincy is represented in the [Massachusetts State Senate](/wiki/Massachusetts_Senate "Massachusetts Senate") by Democrat [John F. Keenan](/wiki/John_F._Keenan_(politician) "John F. Keenan (politician)") ([Norfolk and Plymouth district](/wiki/Massachusetts_Senate%27s_Norfolk_and_Plymouth_district "Massachusetts Senate's Norfolk and Plymouth district")). Three members of the [Massachusetts House of Representatives](/wiki/Massachusetts_House_of_Representatives "Massachusetts House of Representatives") represent Quincy: [Bruce Ayers](/wiki/Bruce_Ayers "Bruce Ayers") ([1st Norfolk district](/wiki/Massachusetts_House_of_Representatives%27_1st_Norfolk_district "Massachusetts House of Representatives' 1st Norfolk district")), [Tackey Chan](/wiki/Tackey_Chan "Tackey Chan") ([2nd Norfolk district](/wiki/Massachusetts_House_of_Representatives%27_2nd_Norfolk_district "Massachusetts House of Representatives' 2nd Norfolk district")), and [Ronald Mariano](/wiki/Ronald_Mariano "Ronald Mariano") ([3rd Norfolk district](/wiki/Massachusetts_House_of_Representatives%27_3rd_Norfolk_district "Massachusetts House of Representatives' 3rd Norfolk district")).[[83]](#cite_note-83) Each representative is a Democrat, and Mariano is the speaker of the House.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/MunrohallENC.jpg/330px-MunrohallENC.jpg)](/wiki/File:MunrohallENC.jpg)

Munro Hall on the Eastern Nazarene College main campus

Quincy is home to various educational institutions, public and private, including a [Montessori school](/wiki/Montessori_education "Montessori education"), a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") academy, and one independent [college-preparatory school](/wiki/College-preparatory_school "College-preparatory school"). [Eastern Nazarene College](/wiki/Eastern_Nazarene_College "Eastern Nazarene College"), a private liberal arts and sciences college that is currently in the process of closing after the 24–25 school year,[[85]](#cite_note-:2-85) and [Quincy College](/wiki/Quincy_College "Quincy College"), a public community college, two public [high schools](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school"), five public [middle schools](/wiki/Middle_school "Middle school"), and 12 public [elementary schools](/wiki/Elementary_school "Elementary school"). In the 19th century, the city became an innovator in progressive public education with the [Quincy Method](/wiki/Quincy_Method "Quincy Method"), developed by [Francis W. Parker](/wiki/Francis_Wayland_Parker "Francis Wayland Parker") while he served as Quincy's [superintendent of schools](/wiki/Superintendent_(education) "Superintendent (education)"). Four years after its implementation, a state survey found that Quincy students excelled at reading, writing, and spelling, and ranked fourth in their county in math.[[20]](#cite_note-216.239.59.132-20)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Higher education")]

The city is home to [Eastern Nazarene College](/wiki/Eastern_Nazarene_College "Eastern Nazarene College"), a former college of the liberal arts and sciences located in [Wollaston Park](/wiki/Wollaston_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Wollaston (Quincy, Massachusetts)"). The college relocated to the area in 1919 from its original location in [Saratoga Springs, New York](/wiki/Saratoga_Springs,_New_York "Saratoga Springs, New York"), where it was established as a "[holiness](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement") college" in 1900. In June 2024, The Board of Trustees of [Eastern Nazarene College](/wiki/Eastern_Nazarene_College "Eastern Nazarene College") announced that the institution was preparing a plan to take steps toward closure, with the goal of closing the college at the end of the 2024–2025 school year.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[85]](#cite_note-:2-85) The future of its campus is uncertain as of November 2024.

[Quincy College](/wiki/Quincy_College "Quincy College"), a [community college](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") in [Quincy Center](/wiki/Quincy_Center "Quincy Center"), operates under the auspices of the City of Quincy. The college is unusual in this respect, as it is the only one of Massachusetts' 16 community colleges to be run by a city rather than by the [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state").[[87]](#cite_note-87) It is one of only two colleges in the United States organized this way.[[88]](#cite_note-gratz-88)

### Private and alternative education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Private and alternative education")]

Private and alternative education institutions for children in preschool through 8th grade include Quincy's three Catholic parochial schools — Sacred Heart, St. Ann, and St. Mary.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) The [Archdiocese of Boston](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Boston "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Boston") decided to merge these three schools to form the Quincy Catholic Academy, which opened in 2010 on the site of the former Sacred Heart school.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

The [Woodward School for Girls](/wiki/Woodward_School_for_Girls "Woodward School for Girls"), opened in 1894, is an [independent school](/wiki/Independent_school "Independent school") offering a [college-preparatory education](/wiki/College-preparatory_school "College-preparatory school") to girls in grades 6–12.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

The Adams Montessori School is open to children of preschool through elementary school age.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Public libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Public libraries")]

The [Thomas Crane Public Library](/wiki/Thomas_Crane_Public_Library "Thomas Crane Public Library") serves as the flagship library of the city's library network, which is part of the regional [Old Colony Library Network](/wiki/Old_Colony_Library_Network "Old Colony Library Network").

### Supplementary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Supplementary education")]

Several Chinese community organizations in Quincy have offered after-school and weekend instruction in [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") to local youth over the years. The Quincy Chinese Language School and The Chung Yee School are no longer in operation. In 2016, The South Shore Chinese Language School began offering popular weekend classes in Mandarin and Cantonese to children ages 5 and up at the Quincy YMCA.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8f/QuincyPresidents.jpg/250px-QuincyPresidents.jpg)](/wiki/File:QuincyPresidents.jpg)

City of Presidents banner previously displayed on Route 3A. The temporary [Fore River Bridge](/wiki/Fore_River_Bridge "Fore River Bridge") can be seen in the background.

As part of [Metro Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston"), Quincy has easy access to transportation facilities. State highways and the Interstate system connect the [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") area to the airport, port, and intermodal facilities of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"). Due to its proximity to Boston proper, Quincy is connected not only by these modes of transportation but also to the regional [subway](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system, operated by the [Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Transportation_Authority "Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority") (MBTA), known locally as "The T". The four subway or "T" stops in Quincy, which are on the MBTA's [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(MBTA) "Red Line (MBTA)"), are [North Quincy Station](/wiki/North_Quincy_(MBTA_station) "North Quincy (MBTA station)"), [Wollaston Station](/wiki/Wollaston_(MBTA_station) "Wollaston (MBTA station)"), [Quincy Center Station](/wiki/Quincy_Center_(MBTA_station) "Quincy Center (MBTA station)"), and [Quincy Adams Station](/wiki/Quincy_Adams_(MBTA_station) "Quincy Adams (MBTA station)").

### Highways and roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Highways and roads")]

[Interstate 93](/wiki/Interstate_93 "Interstate 93") and [U.S. Route 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_in_Massachusetts "U.S. Route 1 in Massachusetts") travel south to north concurrently through Quincy beginning in the southwest, where the Quincy–Randolph border bisects the median between the northern and southern halves of the Exit 5 cloverleaf at Massachusetts Route 28. Following a route around the southern extent of the [Blue Hills Reservation](/wiki/Blue_Hills_Reservation "Blue Hills Reservation"), this I-93 and US 1 alignment is along the former southern section of [Route 128](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_128 "Massachusetts Route 128"). The highway travels along a wooded wetland region of the Reservation, entering Quincy completely just beyond Exit 5 and then crossing into Braintree as it approaches the [Braintree Split](/wiki/Braintree_Split "Braintree Split"), the junction with [Massachusetts Route 3](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3 "Massachusetts Route 3"). Weekday traffic volume averages 250,000 to 275,000 vehicles per day at this intersection, the gateway from Boston and its inner core to the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Massachusetts) "South Shore (Massachusetts)") and [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod").[[97]](#cite_note-MPO-97)

As Route 3 joins I-93 and US 1 at the Braintree Split, the three travel north together toward Boston around the eastern extent of the Blue Hills Reservation, entering [West Quincy](/wiki/West_Quincy_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "West Quincy (Quincy, Massachusetts)") as the [Southeast Expressway](/wiki/Southeast_Expressway_(Boston) "Southeast Expressway (Boston)"). The expressway provides access to West Quincy at Exit 8—[Furnace Brook Parkway](/wiki/Furnace_Brook_Parkway "Furnace Brook Parkway") and Exit 9—Bryant Avenue/Adams Street before entering Milton. The Furnace Brook Parkway exit also provides access to Ricciuti Drive and the [Quincy Quarries Reservation](/wiki/Quincy_Quarries_Reservation "Quincy Quarries Reservation") as well as the eastern entrance to the [Blue Hills Reservation Parkways](/wiki/Blue_Hills_Reservation_Parkways "Blue Hills Reservation Parkways").

Principal numbered state highways traveling within Quincy include: [Route 3A](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3A "Massachusetts Route 3A") south to north from Weymouth via Washington Street, [Southern Artery](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Artery&action=edit&redlink=1 "Southern Artery (page does not exist)"), Merrymount Parkway and Hancock Street to the Neponset River Bridge and the [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Boston "Dorchester, Boston") section of Boston; [Route 28](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_28 "Massachusetts Route 28"), which travels south to north from Randolph to Milton along Randolph Avenue in Quincy through a remote section of the Blue Hills Reservation; and [Route 53](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_53 "Massachusetts Route 53"), which enters traveling south to north from Braintree as Quincy Avenue, turning right to form the beginning of Southern Artery in [Quincy Point](/wiki/Quincy_Point_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Quincy Point (Quincy, Massachusetts)") before ending at the intersection with Washington Street/Route 3A.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Quincycenter.jpg/250px-Quincycenter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quincycenter.jpg)

Quincy Center as seen from the intersection of Adams Street and Hancock Street

In addition to the Blue Hills [parkways](/wiki/Parkway "Parkway"), Quincy includes two other [Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation](/wiki/Massachusetts_Department_of_Conservation_and_Recreation "Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation") parkways. [Furnace Brook Parkway](/wiki/Furnace_Brook_Parkway "Furnace Brook Parkway") travels east from I-93 through the center of the city from West Quincy to [Quincy Center](/wiki/Quincy_Center_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Quincy Center (Quincy, Massachusetts)") and [Merrymount](/wiki/Merrymount_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Merrymount (Quincy, Massachusetts)") at [Quincy Bay](/wiki/Quincy_Bay "Quincy Bay"). There the parkway meets [Quincy Shore Drive](/wiki/Quincy_Shore_Drive "Quincy Shore Drive") at the mouth of Blacks Creek. Quincy Shore Drive travels in a northerly direction along the shore of Quincy Bay through [Wollaston](/wiki/Wollaston_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Wollaston (Quincy, Massachusetts)") and into [North Quincy](/wiki/North_Quincy_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "North Quincy (Quincy, Massachusetts)"), with much of its length abutting [Wollaston Beach](/wiki/Wollaston_Beach "Wollaston Beach"), then turns in a westerly direction upon intersecting with East Squantum Street and continues to meet Hancock Street at the Neponset River Bridge.

As for Quincy's other important city streets, Hancock Street begins at the southern extent of Quincy Center as a continuation of Quincy Avenue and travels north to Dorchester as a main commercial thoroughfare of Quincy Center, Wollaston and North Quincy. Washington Street enters the city at Fore River Rotary after crossing [Weymouth Fore River](/wiki/Weymouth_Fore_River "Weymouth Fore River") via the [Fore River Bridge](/wiki/Fore_River_Bridge "Fore River Bridge") and continues to Quincy Center, ending at Hancock Street. Along with Quincy Avenue and Southern Artery, other heavily traveled streets include Newport Avenue, which parallels Hancock Street to the west on the opposite side of the MBTA railway, Adams Street heading west from Quincy Center to Milton, and West and East Squantum Streets in the [Montclair](/wiki/Montclair_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Montclair (Quincy, Massachusetts)") and North Quincy neighborhoods. Other streets are discussed in several of the neighborhood articles listed above.

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Airport")]

Boston's [Logan International Airport](/wiki/Logan_International_Airport "Logan International Airport") is accessible via MBTA Red Line connections at [South Station](/wiki/South_Station "South Station"), directly on the MBTA commuter boat (see below) or by motor vehicle using [Interstate 93](/wiki/Interstate_93 "Interstate 93") or surface roads to the [Ted Williams Tunnel](/wiki/Ted_Williams_Tunnel "Ted Williams Tunnel").

### MBTA rail and other commuter services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: MBTA rail and other commuter services")]

Subway service is available on the [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(MBTA) "Red Line (MBTA)") of the MBTA from four stations in Quincy: [North Quincy](/wiki/North_Quincy_station "North Quincy station"), [Wollaston](/wiki/Wollaston_station "Wollaston station"), [Quincy Center](/wiki/Quincy_Center_station "Quincy Center station"), and [Quincy Adams](/wiki/Quincy_Adams_station "Quincy Adams station"). Commuter rail service operates out of Quincy Center. Both services serve [South Station](/wiki/South_Station "South Station") in Boston with connections to [MBTA Commuter Rail](/wiki/MBTA_Commuter_Rail "MBTA Commuter Rail") and [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") intercity lines. Buses are also available for transportation in Quincy, including private bus lines and several lines provided by the MBTA. Most of the MBTA routes funnel through the Quincy Center station, which is the principal hub south of Boston for all MBTA bus lines. The southern bus garage for the MBTA system is adjacent to the Quincy Armory on Hancock Street.

[Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard") in [Quincy Point](/wiki/Quincy_Point "Quincy Point") was formerly a terminal for the [MBTA ferry](/wiki/MBTA_ferry "MBTA ferry") system.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Service ended in October 2013 after a water main break damaged the sea wall and wharf. Temporary repairs would have cost $15 million; permanent repairs $50 million. In 2014, the MBTA made the decision to permanently end the service and sell the land.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quincy,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sports")]

Quincy has had brief flirtations with professional sports. The Quincy Chiefs of the minor league Eastern Basketball Association (the predecessor to the defunct [Continental Basketball Association](/wiki/Continental_Basketball_Association "Continental Basketball Association")) played a single season in 1977–1978, and were coached and managed by former [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") executive [Leo Papile](/w/index.php?title=Leo_Papile&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leo Papile (page does not exist)"). The Chiefs finished 12–19 in third place, and lost in the playoffs to eventual league champion [Wilkes-Barre](/wiki/Wilkes-Barre,_Pennsylvania "Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania"). Quincy's professional baseball team, the Shipbuilders, competed in the [New England League](/wiki/New_England_League "New England League") in 1933, recording a 12–6 record before moving to [Nashua](/wiki/Nashua,_New_Hampshire "Nashua, New Hampshire") midseason; a revival of the team played in the NEL from 1941 through to 1944. The final season of the [Boston Minutemen](/wiki/Boston_Minutemen "Boston Minutemen") of the [North American Soccer League](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%9384) "North American Soccer League (1968–84)") was played at [Veterans Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Memorial_Stadium_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Veterans Memorial Stadium (Quincy, Massachusetts)"), in 1976, finishing 7–17.

The [Real Boston Rams](/wiki/Boston_Rams "Boston Rams") of the [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") 4th division [Premier Development League](/wiki/USL_League_Two "USL League Two"), an affiliate club of the [New England Revolution](/wiki/New_England_Revolution "New England Revolution"), played in Veterans Memorial Stadium from 2014 to 2015.

In 2019, the [Boston Cannons](/wiki/Boston_Cannons "Boston Cannons"), a professional men's [field lacrosse](/wiki/Field_lacrosse "Field lacrosse") team in the [Premier Lacrosse League](/wiki/Premier_Lacrosse_League "Premier Lacrosse League") (PLL) moved their home stadium from Boston to Veterans Memorial Stadium. The team played two seasons there before the [MLL–PLL merger](/wiki/MLL%E2%80%93PLL_merger "MLL–PLL merger").

The [New England Free Jacks](/wiki/New_England_Free_Jacks "New England Free Jacks") of [Major League Rugby](/wiki/Major_League_Rugby "Major League Rugby") moved to Veterans Memorial Stadium in 2021, relocating from Weymouth.

Quincy has had several [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") teams in the semi-pro Eastern Football League over the years. The current club, the Quincy Militia, played its inaugural season in the EFL in 2009.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Founded in 2009 by long-time Quincy resident Vaughn Driscoll, new owners came into the team picture in 2013. Militia games are played July to October with home games at Veterans Memorial Stadium on Saturday nights. An earlier team, the Quincy Giants, played in the minor league [Atlantic Coast Football League](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Football_League "Atlantic Coast Football League") between 1969 and 1971.

Quincy's only college sports program was the "Lions" of [Eastern Nazarene College](/wiki/Eastern_Nazarene_College "Eastern Nazarene College"), in the [D-III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") [Conference of New England](/wiki/Conference_of_New_England "Conference of New England") of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) and the [Eastern College Athletic Conference](/wiki/Eastern_College_Athletic_Conference "Eastern College Athletic Conference") (ECAC), before the closure of the college in June 2025.

Quincy's high school sports programs are in the Patriot League:[[101]](#cite_note-101) the D-III Fisher Division "Raiders" of [North Quincy High School](/wiki/North_Quincy_High_School "North Quincy High School") and the DIIA Keenan Division "Presidents" of [Quincy High School](/wiki/Quincy_High_School_(Massachusetts) "Quincy High School (Massachusetts)"), who are rivals. Quincy also hosted the youth baseball [Babe Ruth League](/wiki/Babe_Ruth_League "Babe Ruth League") World Series in 2003, 2005 and 2008. High school baseball and Babe Ruth League games are played at Adams Field. High school football is played at [Veterans Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Memorial_Stadium_(Quincy,_Massachusetts) "Veterans Memorial Stadium (Quincy, Massachusetts)")
